topRanked.articleTitle: The bats of the Congo and of Rwanda and Burundi revisited (Mammalia: Chiroptera)
topRanked.articleAuthor: Cakenberghe, Victor Van, Tungaluna, Guy-Crispin Gembu, Akawa, Prescott Musaba, Seamark, Ernest & Verheyen, Erik
topRanked.speciesDesc: Laephotis angolensis is a subspecies of leopard with distinct cranial characteristics, making it different from Laephotis wintoni angolensis. It was previously considered monotypic but has since been found to be specifically different based on cranial features. The species is known from Angola, Zambia, Botswana, and the southern part of the Democratic Republic of Congo.

topRanked.articleAuthor: Don E. Wilson & Russell A. Mittermeier
topRanked.journalTitle: Handbook of the Mammals of the World – Volume 9 Bats
topRanked.speciesDesc: Laephotis botswanae, also known as the Botswana Long-eared Bat, is a species of bat found in eastern and southern Africa. It has distinctive reddish-brown coloration with blackish-brown tips on its hairs, particularly on the ventral region. The species is characterized by its soft, shaggy pelage, pale to medium brown ears, and dark brown wings. Laephotis botswanae inhabits riverine woodland or woodland savanna habitats near water sources, including streams, lakes, and marshes.

response.answer: Laephotis botswanae is distributed in extreme southern DR Congo, southwestern Tanzania, southwestern Angola, Zambia, Malawi, northeastern Namibia, northwest Botswana, Zimbabwe, and northeast South Africa. According to Document 1, it is commonly found over or close to water, in riverine woodland or woodland savanna.
